questions.text: I tend to feel the emotional stakes of a situation far more intensely than the practical stakes.
I find that I offer my most effective support when I can separate the emotional dimension of a situation from the practical one.
I find that conversations seem layered to me, with a surface meaning and a deeper one running simultaneously.
I tend to match the pace others have set rather than impose my own.
I detect when a person uses physical touch, like a pat on the back, to establish a hierarchy rather than express warmth.
I tend to focus on the task at hand rather than the interpersonal dynamics around it.
My internal experience is like a symphony played at full volume — nothing is muted.
I take pride in my ability to think on my feet and handle the unexpected.
I skim agreements and sign quickly, trusting I'll address issues if they arise.
I find that I gravitate toward the choice that most benefits my personal situation.
I believe that decisions made under emotional pressure are more likely to be biased than those made with deliberate calm.
I feel that breaking routines is what opens the door to creative breakthroughs.
I believe that emotional steadiness in the midst of others' pain is an achievement that benefits both me and the people I am trying to help.
I feel that a clear chain of command produces better results.
I believe my system is wired for resilience — it bends under pressure but returns to form quickly.
I believe that if something is important, it should be stated explicitly.
When something touches me deeply, I can feel it reverberating in my chest for hours.
I am the person who asks the team how they would like to frame a problem.
I believe that the moral weight of suffering is the reality that prevents ethical reasoning from becoming a game of abstract logic.
I am recognized for my ability to rally people behind a single vision.
I routinely make financial sacrifices to support people in my life.
I hold the view that if I can solve a problem for someone else, I am obligated to try.
I hold the view that explicit communication is a kindness, not a lack of sophistication.
I periodically declutter my spaces to keep things functional and clear.
I hold the view that generosity should come before security.
I feel responsible for creating the conditions that will lead to my desired future.
I tend to describe my emotional experiences in moderate terms — things are good, fine, okay.
I notice that my hands stay steady and my voice stays even in situations that rattle other people.
I feel that building equity in my own ventures is smarter than building equity for someone else.
I believe that the weight I assign to suffering in my moral calculus is what determines the shape and direction of my life.
I hold the view that spending on personal health—supplements, checkups, therapy—is never excessive.
I think it is important to remain open and willing to shift with the group.
I pick up on the way someone subtly angles their body toward the person they most want approval from.
I feel most like myself when I am running the show.
I find that the moral weight of others' pain is the single most reliable compass I have for making ethical decisions.
I value knowing exactly where I stand on a project at all times.
